section.title: Stand Firm
section.sectionRationale: After the sobering account of the man of lawlessness and those who perish, Paul turns to the church with thanksgiving and comfort. Over against the deceived stand the beloved, whom God chose from the beginning for salvation through the Spirit and faith in the truth. Therefore they are to stand firm and hold the apostolic teaching, upheld by a prayer that the Lord and the Father would strengthen their hearts. This short movement is the reassuring counterpart to the warning that precedes it.
section.thesis: Because God chose the Thessalonians from the beginning and called them through the gospel to share Christ's glory, they can and must stand firm and hold fast the truth they were taught, resting in the God who by grace gives eternal comfort and strengthens His people for every good word and deed.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the thanksgiving for God's electing, calling, saving work that sets the beloved apart from the perishing, with the resulting command to stand firm and hold the traditions; and the benediction that grounds their steadfastness in the comforting, strengthening grace of the Lord Jesus and the Father.

verses.text: But we should always thank God for you, brothers who are loved by the Lord, because God {{haireomai|chose}} you from the beginning to be saved by the {{hagiasmos|sanctification}} of the Spirit and by faith in the truth.
To this He called you through our gospel, so that you may share in the glory of our Lord Jesus Christ.
Therefore, brothers, {{steko|stand firm}} and cling to the {{paradosis|traditions}} we taught you, whether by speech or by letter.

units.label: Benediction of comfort and strength
units.note: the Lord and the Father encourage and strengthen

verses.text: Now may our Lord Jesus Christ Himself and God our Father, who by grace has loved us and given us eternal {{paraklesis|comfort}} and good hope,
encourage your hearts and strengthen you in every good word and deed.

haireomai.gloss: To choose, select, take for oneself. Paul says God chose the Thessalonians from the beginning for salvation, an act of God's free love that stands behind their faith. The middle voice underlines that God took them to be His own, so their standing rests on His choice, not their strength.

hagiasmos.gloss: Sanctification, the process of being made holy and set apart for God. Here it is the Spirit's work, paired with faith in the truth, as the means by which God's chosen are saved. Salvation is not bare pardon but a real setting apart, begun and carried on by the Holy Spirit.

steko.gloss: To stand firm, hold one's ground, remain steadfast. Built from the verb to stand, it pictures a soldier or a settled person who will not be moved. Because God has chosen and called them, the command is not to muster their own courage but to keep standing in the truth they have received.

paradosis.gloss: Tradition, that which is handed over or passed on. Here it means the apostolic teaching Paul delivered, whether spoken or written, not later human inventions. To hold the traditions is to cling to the authoritative gospel and instruction the apostles gave, the anchor against every deceiving message.

paraklesis.gloss: Comfort, encouragement, exhortation; a calling alongside to help. Paul says the Lord and the Father have given eternal comfort by grace, and prays that this comfort would steady their hearts. It is not fleeting reassurance but a lasting encouragement rooted in God's love and good hope.

basic.context: Having just described those who perish because they refused the truth, Paul turns to the church with relief and gratitude. You are different, he says: God loved you and chose you from the beginning to be saved, set apart by the Spirit and holding faith in the truth, and He called you through the gospel to share in Christ's glory. So the response is simple and strong: stand firm and hold on to what you were taught. Then he prays that God Himself would comfort and strengthen them.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul grounds the command to stand firm in God's prior choosing and calling, so human perseverance rests on divine grace, not the reverse. God's election does not cancel faith and sanctification but works through them; the passage names both God's choice and their faith in the truth. The traditions to be held are specifically apostolic teaching, spoken or written, not any later human custom.
basic.originalLanguages: God haireomai, chose, the Thessalonians from the beginning, an act of free love. Their salvation comes by the hagiasmos of the Spirit, the Spirit's setting apart, together with faith in the truth. Therefore they must steko, stand firm, and hold the paradosis, the apostolic traditions handed down by speech or letter. The benediction rests on paraklesis, the eternal comfort God has given by grace.
basic.typology: God choosing a people from the beginning and setting them apart by His Spirit echoes His election of Israel as His treasured people, set apart to be holy. The church is the chosen and sanctified people, now called through the gospel to share the glory of the Lord Jesus.
basic.christ: Christ is the goal of God's choosing: He called them through the gospel so that they may share in the glory of our Lord Jesus Christ. And in the benediction, the Lord Jesus Christ Himself stands alongside God the Father as the source of comfort and strength, named first, and joined with the Father under a single verb, a quiet witness to His deity.
basic.god: God is the loving Chooser and faithful Caller. He loved this church, chose them from the beginning, and called them to glory, and by grace He gives eternal comfort and good hope. The God who elects is the God who then strengthens His people for every good word and deed, so His grace both begins and sustains the Christian life.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians 1 says God chose us in Christ before the foundation of the world to be holy. First Thessalonians 5 urges the same church to encourage and build one another up. And 1 Peter 1 speaks of a people chosen and sanctified by the Spirit for obedience, the same pattern of election through sanctification.
basic.oneStory: The gospel is the story of a God who chooses, calls, sanctifies, and keeps a people for His glory. Set against the ruin of those who refused the truth, the beloved are held secure by God's prior love, called to share the glory of Christ, and strengthened by grace to stand firm to the end.

context.literary: The word But at the start of verse 13 turns sharply from the perishing of verses 9 to 12 to the beloved church, setting election and salvation against deception and ruin. Thanksgiving, verses 13 and 14, grounds the command of verse 15, and the benediction of verses 16 and 17 seals it in prayer. The passage is the hinge between the doctrinal correction and the practical exhortations of chapter 3.
context.historical: A church rattled by a false message about the end needed both correction and reassurance. By anchoring them in God's electing love and the apostolic teaching they had received in person and by letter, Paul gives them a stable place to stand amid confusion, and a criterion, the traditions handed down by the apostles, for testing every new claim.
context.canonical: The passage joins the New Testament's teaching on election, calling, and sanctification, closely echoing Ephesians 1 and 1 Peter 1. Its pairing of God's sovereign choice with the call to stand firm and hold the truth reflects the consistent biblical rhythm of grace grounding responsibility.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To comfort and stabilize the church by reminding them that God chose, called, and is saving them for Christ's glory, and on that basis to command them to stand firm and hold the apostolic teaching, sealing it with a prayer that God would strengthen their hearts for every good word and deed.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses 13 and 14 describe what God has done, chosen, called, and is saving them. Verse 15 is the prescriptive heart: stand firm and hold the traditions. The command rests on the description; because God has secured them, they are to persevere, and the closing benediction shows that even their obedience depends on God's strengthening grace.

debates.question: Does God's choosing from the beginning mean salvation is fixed apart from faith?

views.label: Election and faith belong together
views.case: The same verse names both God's choice from the beginning and salvation by the Spirit's sanctification and faith in the truth; God's choice is worked out through, not around, real faith and holiness.

views.label: God's choice is the ground, faith the means
views.case: God's electing love stands first and secures the outcome, while faith in the truth is the God-given means by which His choice comes to pass; the two are not rivals but a single saving work.

debates.bearing: Either way the passage gives assurance without fatalism: believers rest in God's prior choice yet are summoned to stand firm and keep faith in the truth.

debates.question: What are the traditions the church is to hold in verse 15?

views.label: The apostolic gospel and teaching, spoken or written
views.case: Paul defines them as what we taught you, whether by speech or by letter, so they are the authoritative apostolic message, now largely preserved in Scripture, not later human customs.

views.label: A warrant for a broader church tradition
views.case: Some appeal to this verse for the authority of unwritten church tradition; but the context ties the traditions specifically to what the apostles delivered, the safeguard against the deceiving messages just described.

debates.bearing: The command anchors the church to the apostolic word as the fixed standard, the very thing that protects them from being shaken by false claims.

study.originalLanguages: The sharp But of verse 13 sets the beloved against the perishing, and the reason for thanksgiving is that God haireomai, chose, them from the beginning, the middle voice stressing that He took them for Himself in free love. Their salvation comes by the hagiasmos of the Spirit, a real setting apart unto holiness, joined with faith in the truth, so grace and faith are one saving movement. On that ground the command lands: steko, stand firm, a word picturing a settled, immovable stance, and hold the paradosis, the apostolic traditions handed over by speech or letter, the fixed standard against deception. The benediction rests everything on paraklesis, the eternal comfort God has already given by grace, praying that this same comfort would steady their hearts and strengthen them for every good word and deed.

typology.type: A people chosen and set apart by God
typology.fulfillment: Takes up God's election of Israel as His treasured, holy people, now fulfilled in the church chosen from the beginning and sanctified by the Spirit for the glory of Christ.

typology.type: Called to share the glory of the Lord
typology.fulfillment: Fulfils the hope of God's people sharing His glory, realised as believers are called through the gospel to share in the glory of the Lord Jesus Christ.

study.christ: Christ is both the goal and the giver in this passage. God's whole saving purpose aims at this, that His chosen people may share in the glory of our Lord Jesus Christ. And when Paul prays, he names our Lord Jesus Christ Himself first, alongside God our Father, as the one who has loved us and given eternal comfort and good hope, and joins them under a single verb, encourage your hearts. To place the Son beside the Father as the shared source of grace and comfort is a quiet but real confession of Christ's deity.
study.god: God is the loving initiator of salvation. He set His love on this church, chose them from the beginning, called them through the gospel, and is saving them by His Spirit for Christ's glory. He is the God who by grace has given eternal comfort and good hope, and who strengthens His people for every good word and deed. His sovereign choice is not cold decree but warm, electing love that secures and sustains His own.

crossReferences.note: God chose us in Christ before the foundation of the world to be holy, the same election unto sanctification Paul names here.
Chosen and sanctified by the Spirit for obedience, matching salvation by the sanctification of the Spirit and faith in the truth.
Encourage one another and build one another up, the same ministry of comfort Paul prays God Himself would give.
Stand firm in the Lord, the same call to steadfastness grounded in belonging to Christ.

study.oneStory: This passage tells the gospel in miniature and sets it in the sweep of the whole story: a God who from the beginning chooses a people, sets them apart by His Spirit, calls them through the gospel, and destines them to share the glory of His Son. Against the dark backdrop of those who refused the truth, the beloved are held by grace, commanded to stand firm on the apostolic word, and carried by the God who both chooses and strengthens His people to the end.

sermon.title: Chosen Before Creation for Salvation
sermon.why: In this Look at the Book study on 2 Thessalonians 2:13-17, Piper traces how God's choosing from the beginning grounds the church's assurance and its call to stand firm, showing that election works through the sanctification of the Spirit and faith in the truth. A careful, orthodox exposition of this movement's heart.

quiz.q: Why does Paul give thanks for the Thessalonians in verse 13?

options.text: Because God chose them from the beginning to be saved through the Spirit's sanctification and faith in the truth.
options.note: Verse 13 grounds the thanksgiving in God's electing, saving work.

options.text: Because they had chosen God by their own unaided will.
options.note: The emphasis falls on God choosing them, not on unaided human choice.

options.text: Because they had earned salvation by their good works.
options.note: Salvation here is by the Spirit and faith, not earned by works.

options.text: Because they were wealthier than the surrounding churches.
options.note: The thanksgiving concerns God's choice and their salvation, not their wealth.

quiz.q: To what did God call the Thessalonians through the gospel, according to verse 14?

options.text: That they may share in the glory of our Lord Jesus Christ.
options.note: Verse 14 names sharing in Christ's glory as the goal of their calling.

options.text: That they may escape all responsibility to obey.
options.note: The next verse commands them to stand firm and hold the truth.

options.text: That they may gain earthly power over others.
options.note: The goal is Christ's glory, not earthly power.

options.text: That they may keep the gospel to themselves.
options.note: They are called to share Christ's glory, not to hoard the message.

quiz.q: What is Paul's central command in verse 15?

options.text: Stand firm and hold fast to the traditions the apostles taught them, by speech or letter.
options.note: Verse 15 commands steadfastness and holding the apostolic teaching.

options.text: Invent new teachings to replace what they received.
options.note: They are to cling to what was taught, not invent new doctrine.

options.text: Wait passively and do nothing at all.
options.note: Standing firm and holding the truth is active perseverance, not passivity.

options.text: Follow whatever new message seems most exciting.
options.note: The whole point is to hold the apostolic teaching against every new claim.

quiz.q: What does the command to stand firm rest upon in this passage?

options.text: God's prior choosing, calling, and saving work, which secures the church.
options.note: The therefore of verse 15 flows from God's electing and calling grace in verses 13 and 14.

options.text: The believers' own willpower and natural courage.
options.note: Their standing rests on God's choice and strengthening, not mere willpower.

options.text: Their success in avoiding all persecution.
options.note: The ground is God's grace, not freedom from hardship.

options.text: The approval of the surrounding culture.
options.note: It rests on God's choosing love, not cultural approval.

quiz.q: What does Paul pray for the church in verses 16 and 17?

options.text: That the Lord Jesus and the Father would comfort and strengthen their hearts for every good word and deed.
options.note: The benediction asks God to encourage and strengthen them by grace.

options.text: That they would be left to strengthen themselves.
options.note: The strengthening comes from the Lord and the Father, not from themselves alone.

options.text: That they would receive material riches and ease.
options.note: The prayer is for comfort, hope, and strength for good works, not riches.

options.text: That they would be freed from doing any good works.
options.note: He prays they be strengthened for every good word and deed, not freed from them.

questions: Paul roots the church's security in God choosing them from the beginning. How does knowing that your salvation began in God's love rather than your effort steady you when your faith feels shaky?
The command to stand firm rests on God's grace, not raw willpower. Where do you most need to simply keep standing in the truth right now, trusting God to hold you?
The church is told to hold fast the apostolic teaching against every deceiving message. What practices help you stay anchored in Scripture when new or exciting claims come along?
God is described as giving eternal comfort and good hope by grace. When have you felt that lasting comfort, and how might you pass it on to someone who is discouraged?
Paul prays that God would strengthen the church in every good word and deed. What specific good word or deed do you sense God strengthening you to do this week?

translationNotes.note: Chose you from the beginning renders one reading of the Greek; a footnoted alternative is God has chosen you as the firstfruits, but either way the stress is on God's electing, saving initiative.
Traditions translates paradosis, that which is handed over; the context defines it as the apostolic teaching delivered by speech or letter, not later human custom.

Our Lord Jesus Christ Himself and God our Father are named as the joint source of comfort, yet the verbs encourage and strengthen are singular, a subtle witness to the unity of the Son with the Father.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Thessalonians 2:13-17. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. All five verses covered contiguously.
